title: Krakow in 48 Hours
intro: Kraków is one of the rare European capitals to have survived the twentieth century with its medieval skin intact, and it rewards the traveler who walks rather than dashes. This weekend stays on foot — Day 1 circles the royal Old Town from Wawel Hill to the great market square, while Day 2 crosses the river into the layered memory and bohemian nightlife of Kazimierz and Podgórze. Two days, eight stops, and a thousand years of Polish history underfoot.

days.label: Day 1
days.subtitle: Wawel Hill, the Royal Route & a Vodka Nightcap

blocks.title: Kings, Tapestries and a Dragon by the River
blocks.body: Begin on Wawel Hill the moment the gates open, while the arcaded Renaissance courtyard is still cool and empty of tour groups. The State Rooms unfurl Flemish tapestries, coronation swords and five centuries of regal heritage, and a walk along the ramparts delivers the Vistula glittering below. Don't leave without finding the fire-breathing bronze Wawel Dragon coiled by the riverbank at the foot of the hill.
blocks.tip: Buy timed State Rooms tickets online the night before — daily numbers are capped and the best morning slots vanish first.

blocks.title: A Kraków Institution Since 1876
blocks.body: Walk north up Grodzka Street into the Main Market Square and settle in at Hawełka, which has been feeding Cracovians classic Polish fare on the Rynek for well over a century. Order the bigos — a slow-cooked hunter's stew of sauerkraut and assorted meats — or the veal Kraków-style, with a half-litre of cold Polish lager. The dining room hums with the comfortable confidence of a place that has nothing left to prove.
blocks.tip: Arrive before 1 p.m. in high season to claim a table without a wait; lunch portions are generous, so pace yourself for the afternoon.

blocks.title: The Largest Medieval Square in Europe
blocks.body: Spend the afternoon on the vast cobbled expanse laid out in 1257, ringed by pastel townhouses and threaded with flower stalls and horse-drawn carriages. Step into the Renaissance Cloth Hall arcade for amber and woodcraft, then time your wander to the top of the hour, when the hejnał trumpet call rings out from St. Mary's taller tower and breaks off abruptly mid-note. If the sky turns, the Rynek Underground Museum beneath the square keeps the medieval city alive four metres down.
blocks.tip: Grab a fresh obwarzanek pretzel-ring from one of the blue street carts for a few złoty and eat it as you cross the square.

blocks.title: Goose Under Gothic Vaults, Then Vodka Done Right
blocks.body: Dine right on the square at Szara Gęś, where refined Polish cooking — its signature roast goose, game in seasonal sauces, Polish duck — arrives under elegant Gothic vaults overlooking the Cloth Hall. Afterward, slip a few minutes away to Wódka Café Bar, a tiny, lively room dedicated to the national spirit. Order a flight of house-infused vodkas served ice-cold the traditional Polish way, with pickles on the side.
blocks.tip: Reserve a table in Szara Gęś's vaulted main hall for the best atmosphere; the kitchen leans into game, so come hungry.

days.label: Day 2
days.subtitle: Wartime Memory, the Jewish Quarter & Plac Nowy After Dark

blocks.title: The Factory That Saved a Thousand Lives
blocks.body: Cross the river to Podgórze for the former enamelware works where Oskar Schindler employed and saved more than a thousand Jewish workers during the occupation. The immersive exhibition 'Kraków under Nazi Occupation 1939–1945' walks you through reconstructed streets, tram cars and personal testimonies, anchored by Schindler's own office and its wall of enamel pots. It is dense and moving; allow at least ninety minutes.
blocks.tip: Book timed tickets online well ahead — this is one of Kraków's most popular museums and frequently sells out days in advance.

blocks.title: A Grandmother's Kitchen in the Jewish Quarter
blocks.body: Walk back across the river into Kazimierz for lunch at Marchewka z Groszkiem, a homely retro room whose name means 'carrot with peas' and whose cooking tastes like a Polish grandmother's. Go for the pierogi ruskie, the crispy placki ziemniaczane, or a bowl of sour-rye żurek — honest comfort food at honest prices, just off the tourist trail. It is the kind of unhurried meal that resets you for an afternoon of heavier history.
blocks.tip: Arrive early to beat the lunch rush; the portions are big and the prices among the friendliest in the quarter.

blocks.title: Traces of Memory in Old Kazimierz
blocks.body: Spend the afternoon on foot through Kraków's former Jewish quarter, a once-independent town of cobbled lanes, historic synagogues and the Renaissance tombstones of the Remuh cemetery. Duck into the Galicia Jewish Museum, whose permanent 'Traces of Memory' photographs document surviving fragments of Jewish life across southern Poland. By day the streets fill with antique shops and galleries; by dusk the candlelit bars begin to wake.
blocks.tip: Most synagogues close on Saturdays for Shabbat, so save the heritage sites for Friday or Sunday and the bars for any night.

blocks.title: Zapiekanka at the Okrąglak, Then Bohemian Candlelight
blocks.body: Dinner is street food done the Kraków way: an open-faced zapiekanka loaded with mushrooms, melted cheese and garlic sauce from Endzior, in the round former kosher poultry hall on Plac Nowy. Eat it standing in the square, then drift across to Alchemia, a dark, candlelit warren of bohemian rooms that has anchored Kazimierz nightlife for decades. Settle in with a Polish craft beer and stay for whatever spills out of the live-music room at the back.
blocks.tip: The Okrąglak windows stay open late and the queues move fast — this is a late-night staple, so there's no need to rush dinner.
